tutorial_1.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

HOW TO ANALYZE ALGORITHM COMPLEXITY:
1. Identify basic operations (comparisons, assignments, arithmetic)
2. Count how many times each runs as function of n
3. Express as formula (e.g., n² + 3n + 2)
4. Drop constants and lower-order terms → O(n²)
5. Verify with Master Theorem if recursive

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: Count every operation naively
  Leads to: 5 + 2n + 3n² + n³
  
OPTIMIZATION 1: Group by operation type
  Still track constants but organize by category
  
OPTIMIZATION 2: Asymptotic simplification
  Drop constants: 5 vs 100 → same O() class
  Drop lower-order: n² + 3n → O(n²)
  Focus on dominant term

PRACTICAL VERIFICATION:
  Test with inputs (n=10, 100, 1000)
  Verify time matches O() prediction
  Doubling n → 4x time for O(n²)?

=== DECISION TREE ===

When analyzing code:
  Q1: Are there loops?
    No → O(1) or O(log n) if recursive
    Yes → Q2
  Q2: How deep are nested loops?
    Depth 1 → O(n), O(n log n), or O(n²)
    Depth k → O(n^k)
  Q3: Is recursion involved?
    Yes → Use Master Theorem: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
  Q4: Problem size shrink each iteration?
    By 1 → O(n)
    By 2 → O(log n)

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

Single loop: O(n)
Nested loops depth 2: O(n²)
Loop with div-by-2: O(log n)
Merge sort T(n)=2T(n/2)+n: O(n log n)
Exponential without memoization: O(2^n)
Same with DP/memoization: O(n²) or O(n³)

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

Confusing worst-case with average-case
Forgetting combine/merge cost in D&C
Assuming loop n when actually log n
Not recognizing O(1) preprocessing
Ignoring space complexity

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

Algorithm Definition: Systematic method processing input → output

Key Components:
1. Description (step-by-step)
2. Complexity Claim (time/space)
3. Correctness Claim (right answer)

Complexity Analysis:
- Time: Duration as function of n
- Space: Memory as function of n

Asymptotic Notation:
Big-O (O): Upper bound
Big-Omega (Ω): Lower bound
Big-Theta (Θ): Tight bound

Growth Hierarchy:
O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ)

tutorial_2.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

RECOGNIZE D&C PROBLEMS:
1. Can problem split into independent subproblems?
2. Are subproblems same type as original?
3. Can solutions combine efficiently?
4. Optimal substructure?

SOLVING D&C (4 steps):
Step 1: Identify divide point (usually n/2 or binary)
Step 2: Write recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
  - a = number of subproblems
  - b = factor dividing problem
  - f(n) = work to divide + combine
Step 3: Apply Master Theorem
Step 4: Verify with base cases

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: No divide, O(n) linear scan
BASIC D&C: Split in half, simple combine
  Example: Merge Sort = O(n log n)
OPTIMIZED D&C: Reduce subproblems (not 2, but 1 or 3)
  Example: Binary Search = O(log n)

=== DECISION TREE ===

D&C problem if all YES:
  Q1: Can split input in half?
  Q2: Process halves independently?
  Q3: Combine results easily?
  Q4: Combine cost small relative to problem?

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n) [Merge Sort]
T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1) → O(log n) [Binary Search]
T(n)=3T(n/2)+O(n²) → O(n²·⁵⁹) [Karatsuba]

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

Forgetting combine cost f(n)
Assuming only divide by 2 (can use any b)
Not checking Master Theorem cases carefully
QuickSort worst-case O(n²) with bad pivot
D&C not always better

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

D&C breaks into subproblems, solves recursively, combines
Recurrence: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)

Master Theorem - Let k = log_b(a):
1. f(n)=O(n^(k-ε)) → T(n)=Θ(n^k)
2. f(n)=Θ(n^k log^p n) → T(n)=Θ(n^k log^(p+1) n)
3. f(n)=Ω(n^(k+ε)) → T(n)=Θ(f(n))

Key Algorithms:
Merge Sort: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n = O(n log n)
Binary Search: T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1) = O(log n)
Counting Inversions: O(n log n) via merge sort

tutorial_3.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

DESIGN GREEDY:
1. Identify greedy choice (local optimum)
2. Prove doesn't lose optimality
3. Apply repeatedly
4. Verify with exchange argument

EXCHANGE ARGUMENT (4 steps):
Step 1: OPT = optimal, GREEDY = greedy solution
Step 2: If OPT differs at some choice
Step 3: Swap OPT's choice for GREEDY's choice
Step 4: New still optimal → By induction GREEDY=OPT

WHEN GREEDY WORKS:
✓ Optimal substructure
✓ Greedy choice property (local=global)
✗ When subproblems interfere
✗ When future depends on past

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: Try all selections (2^n)
GREEDY HEURISTIC: Locally optimal choice (might be wrong)
PROVEN GREEDY: Optimal choice with proof
OPTIMIZED: Sorting O(n log n) + greedy O(n)

=== DECISION TREE ===

Greedy problem if:
  Q1: Can make obvious local choice? YES
  Q2: Forget choice and move on (no backtrack)? YES
  Q3: Choices independent? Likely YES
  Q4: Provable via exchange argument? If YES → greedy optimal

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

Pick X repeatedly → Activity selection
Maximize ratio A/B → Fractional knapsack
Merge minimums bottom-up → Huffman coding
Sort then scan left-to-right → Exchange likely works

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

Greedy LOOKS right but unproven
Different criteria very wrong (knapsack by value fails)
Works FRACTIONAL knapsack not 0/1 (need DP)
Fails when future depends on past
US coins make greedy optimal; arbitrary coins do NOT

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

Greedy Choice Property: Local optimum = global optimum
Optimal Substructure: Optimal contains optimal subproblems
Exchange Argument: If OPT differs, swap → still optimal

Key Algorithms:
Activity Selection: Pick earliest finish time, O(n log n)
Fractional Knapsack: Sort value/weight ratio, O(n log n), only fractional
Huffman Coding: Merge lowest frequencies, O(n log n), greedy → optimal tree

tutorial_4.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

SOLVE DP (5 steps):
Step 1: DEFINE STATE - dp[i] or dp[i][j] = what?
Step 2: WRITE RECURRENCE - how does state depend on subproblems?
Step 3: BASE CASES - simplest subproblem solved directly
Step 4: COMPUTE ORDER - fill table bottom-up (small first)
Step 5: EXTRACT ANSWER - which table cell has final answer?

TWO APPROACHES:

MEMOIZATION (top-down):
✓ Intuitive, solves only needed subproblems
✓ Recursive formulation cleaner
✗ Function call overhead
✗ Stack overflow risk
→ Use for: Sparse subproblem set

TABULATION (bottom-up):
✓ Faster (no recursion)
✓ Clear dependency order
✗ Compute ALL subproblems
✗ Determine dependency order
→ Use for: Dense subproblems, competitive programming

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

STAGE 1: Brute force O(exponential)
  fib(n)=fib(n-1)+fib(n-2) recomputes same subproblems
STAGE 2: Add memoization
  Cache results, check before computing
STAGE 3: Convert to tabulation
  Eliminate recursion, fill table iteratively
STAGE 4: Optimize space
  Only need previous state → O(1) space

=== DECISION TREE ===

DP problem if:
  Q1: Recursive overlapping subproblems?
    Same fib(3) computed many times? → YES
  Q2: Optimal substructure?
    Optimal uses optimal subproblems? → YES
  Q3: Can define state uniquely?
    YES → Likely DP

Problem type:
  "Max/min value" → max/min recurrence
  "Count ways" → addition recurrence
  "Can achieve X?" → boolean table

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

Pattern 1: Fibonacci O(n)
  dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2], optimize space to O(1)
Pattern 2: Longest subsequence O(mn)
  dp[i][j] depends on match A[i], B[j]
Pattern 3: Interval/range O(n³)
  dp[i][j] for subarray, try split points
Pattern 4: Knapsack O(nW)
  dp[i][w]=max value using items 1..i
Pattern 5: Counting
  dp[i]=number of ways, addition recurrence

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

State definition unclear → table wrong
Recurrence formula wrong
Base cases missing/wrong
Wrong computation order
Forgetting to extract answer
Space not optimized
0/1 vs unbounded knapsack confusion

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

DP solves overlapping subproblems:
1. Overlapping Subproblems: Reused multiple times
2. Optimal Substructure: Solution uses optimal subproblems

Steps: Define state → Recurrence → Base cases → Order → Extract

Key Algorithms:
Fibonacci: O(n) DP vs O(2^n) brute force
Rod Cutting: O(n²) max revenue
LCS: O(mn) 2D table
0/1 Knapsack: O(nW) each item in or out

tutorial_5.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

2D STATE DEFINITION:
dp[i][j] for two sequences
dp[i][w] for first i items with constraint w
Consider what uniquely identifies subproblem

MEMOIZATION vs TABULATION:

MEMOIZATION:
✓ Easier initially
✓ Only needed subproblems
✗ Recursion overhead
→ Best: Sparse subproblems

TABULATION:
✓ Faster, no recursion
✓ Explicit dependencies
✗ All subproblems
→ Best: Dense, competitive

SPACE OPTIMIZATION:

Pattern 1: Only need previous
  dp[i] depends on dp[i-1]
  Use 2 variables: O(1) space
Pattern 2: Need row above
  Process row-by-row, iterate backwards
Pattern 3: Random access
  Keep full table (no optimization)

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: Recursive, O(exponential)
MEMOIZED: Add cache dictionary
TABULATION: Convert to loops
SPACE-OPTIMIZED: Reduce dimensions

=== DECISION TREE ===

2-SEQUENCE PROBLEMS:
  State: dp[i][j] for first i, j chars
  Patterns: LCS, Edit distance

0/1 KNAPSACK:
  dp[i][w]=max value using items 1..i
  OUTER loop: items
  INNER loop: weights BACKWARD

UNBOUNDED KNAPSACK:
  dp[w]=max value with capacity w
  OUTER loop: weights
  INNER loop: coins FORWARD
  Can reuse coins!

COUNTING PROBLEMS:
  Addition instead of max/min
  dp[w]=sum of dp[w-coin]

INTERVAL PROBLEMS:
  dp[i][j]=answer for range [i,j]
  Try all split points k, O(n³)

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

Pattern 1: Coin Change O(amount·coins)
  Unbounded: iterate coins inner
Pattern 2: Matrix Chain O(n³)
  Split points: dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j]
Pattern 3: Edit Distance O(mn)
  Insert/delete/replace operations
Pattern 4: LIS O(n²)
  dp[i]=length ending at i
Pattern 5: LCS O(mn)
  Match: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]+1

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

Wrong cache key
Forward vs backward iteration → Unbounded forward, 0/1 backward
Off-by-one errors in boundaries
LIS: check ALL previous, not just largest
Matrix chain formula with intermediate results
0/1 vs unbounded VERY different

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

Coin Change: O(amount×coins) min coins
Matrix Chain: O(n³) optimal multiplication order
Edit Distance: O(mn) insert/delete/replace
LIS: O(n²) longest increasing subsequence

tutorial_6.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

COMBINATORIAL DP:
1. What are we counting?
2. Define state: dp[i][s]=count of ways
3. Recurrence: Include or exclude element
4. Base: Empty=1 way
5. Answer: Usually dp[n]

SUBSET PROBLEMS:

Boolean: Can achieve target?
  dp[i][s]=can make sum s using first i
  Recurrence: OR operation

Counting: How many ways?
  dp[i][s]=ways to make sum s
  Recurrence: ADDITION (not OR!)

PARTITION:
  Goal: Split into 2 equal groups
  Method: Check sum even, reduce to subset sum

GRID PATHS:
  dp[i][j]=count paths to (i,j)
  Recurrence: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j]+dp[i][j-1]
  Base: dp[0][j]=1, dp[i][0]=1

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: Try 2^n subsets
BASIC DP: 2D table O(nS)
OPTIMIZED: 1D array O(S) space
FURTHER: Recognize specific structure

=== DECISION TREE ===

Type 1: SUBSET SUM (decision)
  "Can select subset summing to target?"
  Boolean table

Type 2: COUNT SUBSETS
  "How many subsets sum to target?"
  ADDITION not OR

Type 3: PARTITION
  "Split into equal-sum subsets?"
  Even check first, reduce to subset sum

Type 4: GRID PATHS
  "Paths from top-left to bottom-right?"
  dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j]+dp[i][j-1]

Type 5: MAX PATH SUM
  "Max sum path?"
  dp[i][j]=grid[i][j]+max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

Boolean Subset Sum:
  dp[i][s]=dp[i-1][s] OR dp[i-1][s-A[i]]

Count Subset Sum:
  dp[i][s]=dp[i-1][s]+dp[i-1][s-A[i]]

Partition:
  1. Sum even?
  2. Subset sum to sum/2

Grid Paths:
  dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j]+dp[i][j-1]

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

OR vs ADDITION → Wrong answer
Not backward iteration → Overwrites values
Partition missing even check
Grid off-by-one errors
Mixing "count ways" vs "count elements"

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

Subset Sum: Can select subset=target? O(nS)
Count Subsets: How many=target? Addition
Partition: Split into equal groups? Reduce to subset sum
Grid Paths: Reach corner? Add arrivals from above, left

tutorial_7.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

MST USE PROPERTIES, NOT BRUTE FORCE:
Why? All spanning trees=exponential
Why properties? Two theorems guide optimal

TWO THEOREMS:

1. CUT PROPERTY: Min edge crossing any cut in some MST
   Justifies greedy edge addition

2. CYCLE PROPERTY: Max edge in any cycle NOT in any MST
   Justifies skipping edges

SOLVE MST:
Step 1: Want weight only or actual edges?
Step 2: Graph structure (sparse/dense)?
Step 3: Verify with properties

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: Enumerate all trees=exponential

GREEDY JUSTIFIED:

KRUSKAL:
  1. Sort edges O(E log E)
  2. Union-Find track components
  3. Add edge if endpoints different
  Complexity: O(E log E)

PRIM:
  1. Start vertex
  2. Min-heap track min edge to tree
  3. Add min edge, expand
  Complexity: O(E log V)

OPTIMIZATION:
  Union-Find path compression: O(α(V))
  Fibonacci heap: O(E+V log V)
  Sparse → Kruskal, Dense → Prim

=== DECISION TREE ===

Q1: Graph connected?
  No → Infeasible or forest
  Yes → Continue

Q2: Just weight or edges?
  Weight → Either algo
  Edges → Either algo

Q3: Graph density?
  Sparse → Kruskal
  Dense → Prim

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

KRUSKAL:
  Sort edges
  Union-Find init
  For each edge:
    If Find(u)≠Find(v):
      Add to MST
      Union(u,v)

PRIM:
  Start vertex
  Min-heap with distances
  While heap:
    u=extract min
    For neighbor v:
      If dist(u,v)<dist[v]:
        Update dist[v]

UNION-FIND:
  MakeSet: parent[x]=x
  Find: path compression
  Union: by rank

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

Forget Union-Find init
Not checking Find(u)≠Find(v)
Prim with broken decrease-key
All MSTs same edges (ties!)
Disconnected graphs (need forest)

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

MST: Spanning tree with minimum total weight
Cut Property: Min edge crossing cut in some MST
Cycle Property: Max edge in cycle NOT in any MST

Kruskal: O(E log E) sort edges, add if no cycle
Prim: O(E log V) grow from vertex
Union-Find: O(α(V)) amortized track/merge

tutorial_8.topic_context:
=== PROBLEM-SOLVING APPROACH ===

MANY VARIANTS, DIFFERENT ALGORITHMS:
Single-source, non-negative → Dijkstra
Single-source, negative (no cycles) → Bellman-Ford
Single-source in DAG → Topological sort
All-pairs → Floyd-Warshall

RELAXATION: Core operation all algorithms
  if dist[u]+weight(u,v)<dist[v]:
    dist[v]=dist[u]+weight(u,v)
  Tries to improve v through u

SOLVE SHORTEST PATH:
Step 1: Identify constraints
Step 2: Choose algorithm
Step 3: Implement carefully

=== SOLUTION PROGRESSION ===

BRUTE FORCE: Try all paths=exponential

BELLMAN-FORD:
  Relax all V-1 times
  O(VE), works negative weights
  Slow but simple

DIJKSTRA:
  Greedy with priority queue
  O((V+E) log V)
  5-10x faster than Bellman-Ford
  Only non-negative

TOPOLOGICAL (for DAGs):
  Process in order
  O(V+E) fastest!
  Only acyclic graphs

JOHNSON (all-pairs negative):
  Bellman-Ford once to reweight
  Dijkstra from each

=== DECISION TREE ===

SINGLE-SOURCE:
  Q1: Non-negative weights?
    Yes → Dijkstra
    No → Bellman-Ford
  Q2: DAG?
    Yes → Topological+relax (fastest!)

ALL-PAIRS:
  Q1: Negative weights?
    No → Floyd-Warshall or Dijkstra each
    Yes → Johnson or Floyd-Warshall
  Q2: How many vertices?
    V<500 → Floyd-Warshall simpler
    V>500 → Johnson faster

=== COMMON PATTERNS ===

DIJKSTRA O((V+E) log V):
  dist[source]=0
  Min-heap all vertices
  While heap:
    u=extract min
    For neighbor v:
      Relax(u,v)

BELLMAN-FORD O(VE):
  dist[source]=0
  For V-1 times:
    For each edge:
      Relax(u,v)
  Check negative cycle

DAG SHORTEST O(V+E):
  Topological sort
  dist[source]=0
  For each u in order:
    For neighbor v:
      Relax(u,v)

FLOYD-WARSHALL O(V³):
  For k=0 to V:
    For i,j:
      dist[i][j]=min(dist[i][j],
                     dist[i][k]+dist[k][j])

=== COMMON PITFALLS ===

Dijkstra with negative → Wrong answer
Forget dist[source]=0
Priority queue not updated properly
Bellman-Ford when Dijkstra applicable
DAG when graph has cycles
Floyd-Warshall huge V=slow
Forget parent/path tracking
Negative cycle detection missing

=== CURRICULUM NOTES ===

Variants by constraint:
Single vs all-pairs
Negative weights?
DAG vs general?

Relaxation: Try improve dist[v] through u

Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V) non-negative greedy
Bellman-Ford: O(VE) any weights, detects cycles
DAG Shortest: O(V+E) DAGs only (fastest)
Floyd-Warshall: O(V³) all-pairs DP
